The complete file has 15 DIV1, 175 DIV2, 2 DIV3, 356 HEAD, 638 P, 2 LG, 13 L, 1,387 NOTE, and 442 PB elements.

entities.description: A public structured digital text representing Arnold's complete 1871 volume II, including edition title page, notices, contents, prefaces, page milestones, headings, notes, and the four medieval content sections.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text CME00030
notes: TEXT/@LANG is enm, appropriate to the medieval prose; the edition apparatus is modern English and biblical lemmata include Latin.
The encoding follows Arnold's printed pagination through p. 423 and includes all four medieval content sections rather than an excerpt.

rights.basis: The U-M item applies the No Copyright - United States rights statement to the digital text.
notes: This is a United States rights statement, not a worldwide public-domain determination.

bibliographic.citation: Select English Works of John Wyclif, ed. Thomas Arnold, vol. II, Sermons on the Ferial Gospels and Sunday Epistles; Treatises (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1871).
bibliographic.title: Select English Works of John Wyclif

entities.description: The immediate source edition for CME00030: a complete edited volume of ferial Gospel sermons, Sunday Epistle sermons, Vae Octuplex, and Of Mynystris in þe Chirche, with editorial preliminaries and apparatus.
entities.label: Thomas Arnold's 1871 Select English Works of John Wyclif, volume II
notes: Arnold calls Bodley 788 the foundation of his text and says he nearly reproduces its orthography while retaining thorn and yogh, but substitutes v for manuscript u and j for manuscript i where sound requires.
He supplied capitals, paragraphs, punctuation, marginal analyses, biblical references, and verse or sermon numbers because the base manuscript lacked those reading aids.
The printed order keeps the sermon divisions together and relocates Vae Octuplex and Of Mynystris from their manuscript positions after the Sunday Gospel and Proprium Sanctorum sections.
Modern scholarship warns that Arnold examined most manuscripts other than Bodley 788 only cursorily.

rights.basis: The edition was published in Oxford in 1871; current public providers identify the text or file as public domain.
rights.component: printed edition text and typographical content

access.accessMethod: Request complete-object photography or existing files through Bodleian Imaging Services.
notes: Request covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, all leaves, and edges where offered; CME00030's exact manuscript folio ranges require repository or modern-edition mapping.

entities.description: A late-fourteenth-century English parchment codex containing a calendar, the Wycliffite sermon cycle, and an associated treatise; Arnold adopted it as the complete foundation of his edition.

holding.notes: Arnold's siglum A; current MARCO record links a ten-image 35mm-slide selection.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Bodley 788
notes: Arnold says it is complete and accurate and makes it the foundation of his printed text.
Hudson notes that Arnold printed this manuscript while treating most others less fully.
The current catalog describes illuminated initials; a complete current digital surrogate is needed for whole-object verification.

entities.description: An early-fifteenth-century parchment codex of Wycliffite sermons, now substantially imperfect, with Sunday materials and ferial Gospel sermons but without the two associated tracts.

holding.notes: Arnold's siglum E; current catalog states that the manuscript is not available online.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Douce 321
notes: Current catalog evidence places ferial Gospel sermons at ff. 167-254, followed by a table at f. 255; relevant Sunday material survives earlier with major lacunae.
Arnold calls it a good but mutilated witness and cites readings under E; his volume-II preface says it lacks Vae Octuplex and Of Mynystris.

access.accessMethod: Request complete-object photography through Bodleian Imaging Services.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century parchment codex containing Sunday Gospel sermons and Vae Octuplex; Arnold cites its readings as C in the tract.

holding.notes: Arnold's siglum C; current catalog states that the manuscript is not available online.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 314
notes: The exact modern folio locus of Vae Octuplex was not established from the public catalog extract.
The current record describes a fifteenth-century binding.

entities.description: An early-fifteenth-century English vellum codex containing interleaved Sunday and ferial sermons, Of Mynystris, another tract, Vae Octuplex, and an omitted Epistle homily; Arnold cites selected readings as G.

holding.notes: Arnold's siglum G; no digitized content is exposed in the current catalog record.

entities.label: British Library Royal MS 18 B IX
notes: The catalog places interleaved homilies at ff. 1-177, Of Mynystris at ff. 177-185, Vae Octuplex at ff. 191v-194v, and an omitted Epistle homily at ff. 194v-196.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century manuscript containing a complete set of ferial Gospel sermons, seven Proprium sermons, and one Sunday sermon; Arnold cites selected ferial readings as Q.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century parchment codex containing Sunday Gospel, Commune, and Proprium sermons, Of Mynystris, and ferial Gospel sermons. Arnold describes and examined it as K but CME00030 does not cite K readings.

holding.notes: Arnold's siglum K; the catalog exposes a complete 370-canvas IIIF object.

entities.description: An imperfect late-fourteenth- or early-fifteenth-century parchment codex containing extant Sunday Epistle and Sunday Gospel sermons, Vae Octuplex, Commune and Proprium sermons, and the opening of Of Mynystris. Arnold describes it as M but CME00030 does not cite M readings.

holding.notes: Arnold's siglum M; the catalog exposes a complete 180-canvas IIIF object.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS B.4.20
notes: Direct image inspection maps extant Sunday Epistle sermons to ff. 1r-14v, Vae Octuplex to ff. 47v-50r, and the surviving opening of Of Mynystris to f. 80v; ferial Gospel sermons are absent.
The Sunday Epistle section is imperfect at its beginning, and the Of Mynystris text loses its continuation after f. 80v.

physicalDescription.format: bound manuscript codex in two hands, physically imperfect through lost quires
notes: The catalog records 64 and 51 lines per page and multiple missing leaves or quires.

next_steps: Request whole-object photography for each manuscript through Bodleian Imaging Services.
Specify covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, all leaves, and edges where offered; separately map all CME00030 component ranges.
For Bodley 788, reconcile exact modern folio ranges with the Hudson-Gradon edition before selective comparison.
For Douce 321, compare any available mediated microfilm but do not treat it as a public complete color facsimile.
openQuestions.question: Can complete current color facsimiles of Bodley 788, Douce 321, and Laud Misc. 314 be obtained to verify Arnold's base and Bodleian collations directly?

next_steps: Request complete-object photography through British Library Digitisation Services for both shelfmarks.
Include every cover, spine, pastedown, flyleaf, blank, leaf, and edge where offered; map the cataloged component ranges separately.
Record supplied-file rights and terms before caching, then collate only the readings actually relevant to Arnold's apparatus.
openQuestions.question: Can complete color facsimiles of Royal MS 18 B IX and Cotton MS Claudius D VIII be obtained to verify G and I readings, codex structure, and exact CME00030 ranges?

next_steps: Ask each college to confirm current shelfmark, extent, date, content mapping, and any existing microfilm or photography.
Request complete-object images with bindings, endleaves, blanks, all leaves, and edges where offered.
Do not infer exact loci from Arnold's nineteenth-century descriptions or the later witness list alone.
openQuestions.question: What are the current item-level catalog records, exact CME00030 folio ranges, access conditions, and complete reproduction routes for St John's College Cambridge MS C.8 and New College Oxford MS 95?

next_steps: Consult U-M collection documentation, archived item metadata, and project records for initial conversion and publication provenance.
Do not treat the TEMPHEAD review date as the creation date.
Compare the public delivery text and checked-in XML if a stable machine-readable U-M export becomes available.
openQuestions.question: Who first keyed or converted CME00030, when was it first published, and what quality-control transformations preceded the documented 2016 review?

next_steps: Use the complete Hudson-Gradon edition and subsequent scholarship for current textual states, witness groupings, and authorship arguments.
Keep the unified cycle, its possible collaboration, the two related tracts, and their Latin analogues analytically distinct.
Collate direct witness images where available rather than reconstructing a modern stemma from Arnold's sparse apparatus.
openQuestions.question: What authorship, revision, and manuscript relationship model should replace Arnold's Wyclif attribution and base-plus-selected-variants presentation for the sermon cycle and two tracts?

reviewNotes: The edition-defined aggregate, sermon-cycle components, associated tracts, manuscript witnesses, Arnold edition, modern facsimiles, U-M encoding, and repository XML remain distinct layers.
Arnold's confident John Wyclif attribution is preserved as historical evidence but not promoted over modern scholarship on anonymous and possibly collaborative composition.

Only manuscripts whose readings are cited in CME00030 are called collated sources; Trinity B.2.17 (K) and B.4.20 (M) are recorded as consulted or described by Arnold, not silently upgraded to collated sources.
Complete-facsimile negatives are limited to public web access checked on 2026-07-12 and do not imply that onsite access, repository photography, or microfilm is impossible.
The Trinity bundles are complete relative to the provider-exposed canvas sequences and contain covers, endleaves, blanks, all leaves, and adjacent texts, but no separate spine or edge views.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00030 XML is a complete University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding of Thomas Arnold's 1871 volume II of Select English Works of John Wyclif: editorial preliminaries, 116 ferial Gospel sermons, 55 Sunday Epistle sermons, Vae Octuplex, and Of Mynystris in þe Chirche. Arnold founded the text on Bodleian MS Bodley 788 (A), supplied punctuation, capitalization, paragraphing, marginal analyses, biblical references, and sermon numbers, and cited selected readings from Douce 321 (E), Royal 18 B IX (G), St John's C.8 (H), Cotton Claudius D VIII (I), New College 95 (Q), and Laud Misc. 314 (C, for Vae Octuplex). Modern scholarship treats the 294-sermon cycle as unified but its single or multiple authorship as unresolved; the two tracts are related pendants, not demonstrated translations of Wyclif's Latin expositions. Complete scans of Arnold volumes I and II, complete 370-canvas and 180-canvas Trinity facsimiles, and the complete ten-canvas Bodleian public selection are cached. The Bodleian selection has no CME00030 overlap, and complete public surrogates were not found for the base witness or six manuscripts represented in Arnold's selected apparatus, so dated negatives and mediated reproduction routes are recorded.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Wycliffite author or authors
catalogSummary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; Arnold selection published 1871
catalogSummary.region: England; Wycliffite or Lollard textual culture
catalogSummary.title: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume II

catalogingSubject.label: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume II: Sermons on the Ferial Gospels and Sunday Epistles; Treatises
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is Thomas Arnold's 1871 volume-two selection: 116 Middle English sermons on the ferial Gospels, 55 sermons on the Sunday Epistles, Vae Octuplex, and Of Mynystris in þe Chirche. The two sermon groups belong to the larger 294-sermon English Wycliffite cycle; the two tracts are related pendants rather than sermons in that cycle. This edition-defined aggregate is distinct from each medieval component work, every manuscript witness, Arnold's printed edition, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML. Arnold's title page, notices, contents, prefaces, marginal analyses, and notes are editorial apparatus and are not cataloged as medieval component works.

contentParts.label: Sermons on the Ferial Gospels
contentParts.summary: One hundred sixteen sermons, numbered CXXIV-CCXXXIX in Arnold's continuous Gospel sequence, on weekday and special-occasion Gospel readings across the liturgical year.

contentParts.label: Sermons on the Sunday Epistles
contentParts.summary: Fifty-five sermons on the Sunday Epistle readings, ordered through the church year from the first Sunday after Trinity to the final Sunday after Trinity.

contentParts.label: Vae Octuplex
contentParts.summary: An exposition of Matthew 23 that redirects Christ's eight woes against scribes, Pharisees, and hypocrites toward friars and prelates.

contentParts.label: Of Mynystris in þe Chirche
contentParts.summary: A polemical exposition of Matthew 24 on true ministry, the papacy as Antichrist, church property and poverty, persecution, schism, and the church's final trials.

assertion.notes: The range is deliberately broad because Wyclif's sole authorship and Arnold's narrower dating to Wyclif's final years are not established.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century
Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; Of Mynystris reflects the Great Western Schism after 1378
notes: The schism references constrain Of Mynystris but do not establish an exact year or transfer that date automatically to Vae Octuplex.

scope.description: First publication of Arnold's volume-two edited selection

extent.description: The medieval selection comprises 116 ferial Gospel sermons, 55 Sunday Epistle sermons, and two prose tracts. The complete XML has 15 DIV1, 175 DIV2, 2 DIV3, 356 HEAD, 638 P, 2 LG, 13 L, 1,387 NOTE, and 442 PB elements. Both LG elements and all 13 L elements represent modern editorial quotations in a preface or notes, so the medieval forms remain prose.

formStatements.description: All 171 sermons and both tracts are prose. The XML's 13 L elements belong to modern editorial quotations in a preface or notes and do not make the medieval selection mixed-form.

scope.description: Biblical lemmata, liturgical headings, citations, and short phrases embedded in the Middle English prose

notes: TEXT/@LANG='enm' describes the medieval text but not Arnold's modern English apparatus or embedded Latin lemmata.
The XML's title-page attribution to John Wyclif is edition-derived and is not treated as proof of medieval authorship.
The record remains partial because authorship, narrower composition dates, tract-cycle relations, and several source-witness access questions remain unresolved.

next_steps: Use the Hudson-Gradon five-volume edition and later scholarship rather than Arnold's attribution as the critical starting point.
Separate evidence for cycle unity from evidence for one author, multiple authors, revision, or compilation.
Preserve manuscript attributions as reception evidence without converting them into confirmed authorship.
openQuestions.question: What authorship or collaboration model best explains the unified 294-sermon English Wycliffite cycle, and what relationship did John Wyclif have to its composition?

next_steps: Review dating arguments in the complete modern English Wycliffite Sermons edition and later studies.
Evaluate internal Great Schism references in Of Mynystris separately from Vae Octuplex and the sermon cycle.
Do not use witness copying dates as automatic composition dates.
openQuestions.question: Can the sermon groups and each associated tract be dated more narrowly without relying on Arnold's assumption of Wyclif's authorship?

next_steps: Compare placement, rubrics, textual states, and codicological boundaries across all known tract witnesses.
Collate the English tracts with their related Latin Matthew 23 and 24 expositions without assuming translation.
Keep Arnold's description of the tracts as pendants distinct from demonstrated medieval compilation history.
openQuestions.question: Were Vae Octuplex and Of Mynystris composed as pendants to the sermon cycle, attached during transmission, or associated through a more complex shared textual program?

notes: Witness production places and dialects are not silently transferred to the intellectual aggregate.

assertion.notes: Supported as an attribution in the reception and manuscript tradition, not confirmed as a statement of authorship.

scope.description: Traditional attribution of the sermon cycle and its associated tracts as represented by Arnold's volume

notes: The unity of the long cycle is textually significant, but current evidence does not resolve whether one author or collaborators composed it.

scope.description: The two sections of the unified English Wycliffite sermon cycle selected for Arnold's volume II

notes: The tracts are related to Wyclif's Latin expositions but are not translations of them, and their authorship remains unproved.

scope.description: The two associated Matthew 23 and Matthew 24 expositions
The 1871 volume selection, edited texts, supplied punctuation and divisions, marginal analyses, notes, and selected apparatus

notes: This responsibility describes the edition layer and does not imply that Arnold authored the medieval texts.

scope.description: Edition transcription founded on Bodley 788, with a portion of the initial copy prepared by W. F. Cornish and the remainder by William Sorell
Selected variants cited under sigla E, G, H, I, Q, and C; Arnold says many other manuscripts were examined only cursorily

summaries.value: The volume joins two large sections of the English Wycliffite sermon cycle with two polemical biblical expositions. The ferial Gospel sermons and Sunday Epistle sermons explain the readings of the church year in Middle English while repeatedly applying scripture to belief, pastoral practice, clerical conduct, property, religious orders, and reform. Vae Octuplex applies Matthew 23's eight woes to friars and prelates. Of Mynystris in þe Chirche expounds Matthew 24 through an account of true ministry, papal wealth and pride, Antichrist, schism, persecution, and the trials of the church.

The record describes an edition-defined aggregate because CME00030 reproduces Arnold's complete volume II rather than one autonomous medieval work. The 171 sermons remain parts of the larger unified 294-sermon cycle, while Vae Octuplex and Of Mynystris are associated tracts. Modern scholarship supersedes Arnold's confident Wyclif attribution with anonymous and possibly collaborative authorship, without erasing the historical attribution.

notes: This is the edition title of the aggregate represented by CME00030, not an original medieval title for a single work.
titles.value: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume II
Sermons on the Ferial Gospels and Sunday Epistles; Treatises

notes: Arnold explains that the title derives from Laud Misc. 314's rubric for the eight woes against scribes, Pharisees, and hypocrites.
